nativeRange.habitat: Submerged in a wide range of freshwater bodies — rivers, streams, lakes, ponds, and brackish estuaries — typically on sandy or silty substrates with sufficient light penetration. Notably cold-tolerant across much of its temperate North American range.

decorPreferences: Fine sand or fine gravel substrate at least 5 cm deep
Open background area for tall ribbon leaves and runner spread
Root tabs near the crown for heavy root feeding
Tank height of at least 45 cm so adult leaves can stand upright before bending at the surface

goodWith: Pond fish (cold-tolerant — koi-safe pond edges, with mesh protection from grazing)
avoidWith: Goldfish (will eat or uproot ribbon leaves)
Large digging cichlids (oscars, large central american cichlids)
Plant-eating fish (silver dollars, large plecos, large barbs)
Copper-based medications (Vallisneria is sensitive to copper)

summary: Jungle Vallisneria is generally considered one of the easiest fast-growing background plants for tall aquariums, with ribbon-like leaves that routinely reach 60 to 120 cm. It spreads readily via runners and tolerates a wide range of water conditions.
careNotes: Vallisneria americana is the tallest of the commonly-traded Vallisneria species — adult leaves routinely reach 60–120 cm and can grow longer in deep tanks, where they typically bend horizontally along the water surface. It is generally considered one of the easiest aquarium plants, suitable for beginners despite its size demands. As with all Vallisneria, it is critical NOT to bury the crown — plant the rhizome just at or barely above the substrate; burial reliably causes crown rot. Once established it spreads aggressively via stolons (runners) that produce daughter plants across the substrate; in most tanks this requires periodic thinning. CO2 is not required but tends to noticeably improve growth and leaf coloration. The species is a heavy root feeder; root tabs near the crown generally outperform water-column dosing, though water-column iron supplementation is also commonly advised — Vallisneria is reportedly iron-sensitive and tends to show pale or yellowing leaves under iron deficiency. It is also commonly reported as sensitive to liquid carbon (Excel-type glutaraldehyde) and to copper-based fish medications, both of which can cause melt. Native across much of North America, it is unusually cold-tolerant (15–30°C reported, with 20–28°C as the typical aquarium range) and makes an excellent outdoor pond plant in temperate climates. The closely related Vallisneria spiralis is a smaller, mid-height form; V. americana is the species to choose when a tall, jungle-style background is the goal.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Tropica plant-detail pages would not render via WebFetch (returned navigation chrome only). Used Modern Aquarium and Aquarium Co-Op as effective primary sources and Aquarium Breeder + Wikipedia for taxonomic and water-parameter cross-checks.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (8+ inches commonly, doubles 24" tank height), temperature (70–86°F / 21–30°C), light (moderate, tolerates a range), CO2 (not required), growth rate (moderate to fast), placement (midground–background), and propagation (runners with plantlets).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-checked light (low — does not require high lighting), growth rate (fast, new shoots as often as every 3 days), placement (tall background for monster tanks), propagation (runners), and care difficulty (easy).

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder
additional.notes: Cross-checked height (up to 1 m or more), family (Hydrocharitaceae), light (moderate–high, 30–50 PAR), temperature (20–28°C, tolerates 15–30°C), pH (6.5–8.0), gH (4–18), propagation (stolons/runners), and care level (easy). Also noted V. americana propagates somewhat slower than V. spiralis.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Taxonomic and biogeographic cross-check: family Hydrocharitaceae, order Alismatales; native to Canada, US, Mexico, Guatemala, Honduras, Cuba, Dominican Republic, Haiti, Venezuela; tolerates 0–18 ppt salinity in wild estuaries; common names Wild Celery, Tape Grass, Eelgrass, Water-celery.
